taskDescription: Drag the correct terms into the blanks to complete statements about patterns of inheritance in genetics.
textField:
In autosomal dominant inheritance, an affected heterozygous parent has a *50* percent chance of passing the mutant allele to each offspring, and the trait shows *vertical* transmission through every generation. Examples include Marfan syndrome and *achondroplasia*.

Autosomal recessive disorders require *two* copies of the mutant allele for phenotypic expression, and carrier parents who are both *heterozygous* have a 25% risk of having an affected child. *Consanguinity* increases the likelihood of autosomal recessive conditions.

X-linked recessive disorders predominantly affect *males* because they have only *one* X chromosome and cannot be carriers. An affected father can never transmit the condition to his *sons* because he passes his Y chromosome to them. Carrier mothers have a 50% chance of having affected sons.

The phenomenon where a genetic disease becomes more severe in successive generations is called *anticipation*, classically seen in trinucleotide *repeat* expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy and *Huntington's* disease. Variable *expressivity* means the same mutation produces different degrees of severity in different individuals.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the patterns of Mendelian inheritance.
Good effort! Review the details you missed.
